three_body.instability_note: Low instability — ESG score, sector position, and FTSE 100 rank tell a broadly consistent story (21-pt spread).
three_body.resolution: Our resolution: Integrity 92/100 × WEM 80/100 → 'Preferred Sustainability Leader'. Jangani, Date & Tucker (SSRN 5618192, 2026) show driver rankings shift across regime windows (COVID → COP26 → energy crisis). We anchor to verifiable harm metrics (emissions, pay ratios, enforcement records) that are regime-stable — not ESG labels that oscillate with market narrative.

summary_investor: Ørsted A/S is projected to hold steady in its energy peer ranking (1→1 of 5) over 3 months. FTSE 100 position: 93rd→96th percentile. WEM 80/100 — credible sustainability — candidate for overweight in ESG mandates. Placebo Index 0.54 — ESG narrative overstates real performance.
summary_corporate: 12-month sector ranking forecast: hold steady (1→1 of 5). FTSE 100: 93rd→98th percentile. Highest-leverage action: Business Model Resilience (72% materiality) — this single driver has the greatest impact on FTSE 100 ranking improvement.
summary_auditor: Of 4 ESG claims analysed, 3 (75%) are independently verifiable. No directly contradicted claims — disclosure quality above sector average. ESG provider spread: 4 pts — within normal range. Priority: third-party audit of Business Model Resilience measurement methodology.
crowd_consensus: Aggregated signal across investor, corporate, and audit lenses: Leading — overweight candidate; low harm, credible signal. Driver rankings are temporally unstable across market regimes (Jangani et al., SSRN 5618192). The 3-body instability — ESG score, peer rank, FTSE position pulling in different directions — is resolved by WEM (80/100) × Integrity (92/100): regime-stable signals grounded in verifiable harm, not rater methodology.
